title: Letters to the Editor
summary: How can that be? The answer rests in the way bears are hunted here. A few are shot over bait. Still more are shot along salmon streams, which is basically a natural form of baiting. And a big number are shot in the mountains in the spring, when they are out foraging for the first greens of spring, or in the fall, when they are after the last berries of the year. Hunting bears on the alpine tundra is difficult. Bears are usually hard to stalk. As a result, some hunters take long shots at these bears. Long shots maximize the chances of a wounding an animal. The farther you are from your target, the less likely you are to hit it. Gravity pulls a bullet down as it flies. Winds deflect a bullet as it moves through the air. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Summer is the comfortable season of eating and sleeping, putting on the weight necessary for the excitement of fall and the rigors of winter. And fall is the crazy season. Normally solitary animals, moose start coming together in September to battle and breed. Bulls settle into mating areas and begin to scent-urinate on trails and in so-called "pitholes'' -- areas up to 10 feet in diameter where they've scraped away vegetation with their feet, usually down to bare ground at the center. It's all hormonal. Testosterone production and testicular mass in bulls increase in September and peak in early October, according to studies cited in "Ecology and Management of the North American Moose.'' summary: DESCRIPTION: Mainly a fishing community, although the economy also depends on logging and sawmill operations. The cannery and sawmill peaked in World War I. A city government formed in 1922. Excellent pink salmon runs aided growth through the late 1930s, but the industry collapsed in the 1950s due to depleted salmon runs. In 1972, Ed Head built a large sawmill near Klawock six miles from Craig, providing year-round jobs. It was sold in the early 1990s to Viking Lumber. summary: Of 28 calves born to radio-collared cows, eight were still alive after the dangerous 10-day post-calving period, when young calves are easy prey for bears and wolves. Through the summer, another six of the wild-born calves were lost to nature. Farnell said it is generally believed a survival rate of 26 calves per 100 cows is required to stabilize a herd. Any higher and the herd is likely growing, but any lower and it's likely declining, he said. The transboundary Chisana herd spends its winters in the Yukon and summers in Alaska. In the late 1980s and early 1990, when warning bells started going off about the state of the herd, calf survival was as low as six calves for every 100 cows. summary: In oil-rich Alaska, environment and development not mutually exclusive By RACHEL D'ORO, Associated Press Writer ANCHORAGE, Alaska (December 19, 10:05 am AST) - Leigh Ann Bauer, who has lived in Alaska for 12 years, calls herself a "big-time animal lover." She also considers herself "pretty pro-oil development." To many people in Alaska, those two things are not mutually exclusive. In fact, sometimes it seems as if people outside Alaska see a bigger conflict between the environment and oil and gas drilling than those living here do - a phenomenon made clear during the recent debates over exploiting new sources of energy in Alaska as existing stores become tapped out. title: Fog, ice, speed lead to 18-car pileup
summary: KNIK RIVER -- A massive pileup in icy fog on the Knik River Bridge Sunday morning closed the Glenn Highway for several hours as emergency responders worked to clear the chaotic scene. Authorities estimated that a total of 23 cars -- 18 northbound, five southbound -- were involved in a series of chain-reaction wrecks on the fog-shrouded bridge, super-chilled by cold air rising off the Knik River. Witnesses said several people who waved warnings at oncoming drivers prevented the pileup from becoming even worse. Tim Shaw, northbound in a semi tractor-trailer loaded with 30,000 pounds of groceries, said he saw a couple guys waving and managed to pull over before he got to the bridge.
